title: The Agentic Economy: How AI Agents Will Transform the Financial System with Circle Co-Founder and CEO Jeremy Allaire

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Today I know Priors, have Jeremy Allaire, the co founder and CEO of Circle.
We'll be talking about cryptocurrency, AI, agentic payments, AI evolving on the blockchain, and a variety of other topics.
Great.
Well, thank you so much for joining us today.
It's a pleasure to have you.

paragraphs.zh: 今天我请到了 Priors，以及 Circle 的 co founder 和 CEO Jeremy Allaire。我们会聊 cryptocurrency（加密货币）、AI、agentic payments（agent 驱动支付）、AI 在 blockchain（区块链）上的演进，以及其他各种话题。太好了。非常感谢你今天加入我们，很高兴邀请到你。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: It's great to be here.
Thank you.
So maybe we can start with

paragraphs.zh: 很高兴来到这里。谢谢。所以也许我们可以先从——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: you just giving a quick overview of Circle, what you do, how you approach the world, because I think we're going to be talking a lot about stablecoins, crypto, AI, and how all these things tie into sort of the agentic future.
But I'd love to just start with sort of origins of the company, what you all are up to, and we can go from there.

paragraphs.zh: 请你先快速介绍一下 Circle：你们做什么、你们如何看待这个世界。因为我想我们接下来会大量谈到 stablecoins（稳定币）、crypto（加密货币）、AI，以及这些东西如何与某种 agentic future（agent 驱动的未来）联系起来。不过我想先从公司的起源开始聊聊，你们现在在做什么，然后我们再从那里展开。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah, for sure.
So, yeah, Circle's been around for a while.
I co founded the company over yeah, thirteen years ago or so, 2013.
And really at inception, I was really excited about this idea that we could create a protocol for dollars on the internet.

And I had been really excited about what was happening with technologies like Bitcoin, and had been, you know, working on kind of internet infrastructure for a long time, and got really excited like if we had like a protocol for dollars on the internet, that, you know, potentially we could have a way to store and move value, you know, instantly, globally, frictionlessly, at no cost, ultimately.

paragraphs.zh: 好，当然可以。Circle 已经存在一段时间了。我大概是在十三年前，也就是 2013 年，co founded 了这家公司。而在公司创立之初，真正让我感到兴奋的是这样一个想法：我们可以为互联网上的美元创建一种 protocol（协议）。当时我对 Bitcoin 等技术的发展非常兴奋，而且我也长期从事某种互联网基础设施方面的工作，所以我特别激动地想到：如果我们能为互联网上的美元建立一种 protocol，那么理论上我们就可能拥有一种方式，能够在全球范围内即时、无摩擦、最终几乎零成本地存储和转移价值。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: The other idea that we were really excited about back then was this idea of programmable money, and the idea that eventually these networks, blockchains, would become like operating systems, and you could actually have machines that intermediate economic activity and financial activity on the Internet, including like autonomous software machines.
And back then, we didn't have generative AI or anything like that.

But this sort of idea of kind of commoditizing the kind of payment utility layer with like very safe digital dollar digital currencies, and then having like programmability of that with machines that are kind of tamper resistant, can run on the internet, that's what kind of drove the founding of the company.

And the view was like, if we could do that, we could actually improve the financial system, make it safer, make it more accessible, make it more efficient, and kind of derive new utility from money that we haven't had before.
And so that was sort of where

paragraphs.zh: 当时另一个让我们非常兴奋的想法，是所谓 programmable money（可编程货币）。也就是：这些网络、这些 blockchains，最终会变得像 operating systems（操作系统）一样，而你实际上可以拥有一些机器，去中介互联网上的经济活动和金融活动，其中甚至包括 autonomous software machines（自主软件机器）。那时候我们还没有 generative AI（生成式 AI）之类的东西。但这种思路——把支付的 utility layer（效用层）通过非常安全的数字美元数字货币进行 commoditize（商品化/标准化），再让这些具备 tamper resistant（抗篡改）特性的机器对其进行 programmability（可编程化），并让它们运行在互联网上——这正是推动公司创立的核心动力。我们的判断是，如果能做到这一点，我们实际上就能改进金融系统，让它更安全、更易获得、更高效，并且从货币中衍生出我们以前从未拥有过的新 utility（效用）。所以这就是当时的大致出发点。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Why is we the dollar aspect of that important?

So if you look at a lot of the things that happened in cryptocurrency in the early days, it was really about creating things that were divorced from the traditional financial system if possible, were not dollar centric.

So, for example, Bitcoin was in part a response to the great financial crisis, and the view that all sorts of weird bailouts happened there, and therefore we needed some alternative sort of financial infrastructure for the world.

paragraphs.zh: 为什么其中“美元”这个部分很重要？因为如果你看 cryptocurrency 早期发生的很多事情，它们真正想做的是尽可能创造一种脱离传统金融体系的东西，而且不是以美元为中心的。比如说，Bitcoin 在某种程度上就是对全球金融危机的一种回应；当时的看法是，那场危机中出现了各种奇怪的 bailout（纾困/救助），因此我们需要为世界建立某种替代性的金融基础设施。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
So, think, so actually, what's very interesting is like, I believe in kind of Austrian economic thought.
I was studying Austrian economic thought like in the early 1990s for a very long time.
And so, I've been interested in sound money theory.
And actually, it was studying the kind of impact of the global financial crisis that drew me into this, because my view is like there has to be a way to build like a safer financial system.

paragraphs.zh: 对。所以，我觉得，其实很有意思的是：我本人相信某种 Austrian economic thought（奥地利经济学思想）。我从 1990 年代初开始研究 Austrian economic thought，研究了很长时间。因此，我一直对 sound money theory（健全货币理论）很感兴趣。实际上，正是对全球金融危机影响的研究把我带入了这个领域，因为我的看法是：一定存在一种方式，能够构建一个更安全的金融系统。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And the key issue there was I was interested in this idea of full reserve money.
And in some ways, Bitcoin is full reserve money because there is no way to fractionally lend Bitcoin per se.

paragraphs.zh: 而其中的关键问题是，我当时对 full reserve money（全额准备金货币）这个概念很感兴趣。从某种意义上说，Bitcoin 就是一种 full reserve money，因为原则上不存在对 Bitcoin 进行 fractional lending（部分准备金放贷）的方式。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Full reserve money means currency that's backed by something hard behind it, some asset.

paragraphs.zh: 全额准备金货币（full reserve money）指的是背后有某种坚实支撑物、某种资产作担保的货币。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Doesn't necessarily mean it's a hard back.
Full reserve money is different than, say, fractional Full reserve banking, I should say, is different than fractional reserve banking.
And so, you know, back in There was another major economic collapse, which was the Great Depression, the run on all the banks, and all that fun.
And in the 1930s, there was a really big debate about like, what's the right construct for the banking system and the financial system?
And there was a proposal from a group of economists called the Chicago Plan.

paragraphs.zh: 这不一定意味着它的支撑物本身就是“硬资产”。我应该说，全额准备金银行（full reserve banking）不同于部分准备金银行（fractional reserve banking）。你知道，过去还发生过另一次重大的经济崩溃，也就是 Great Depression，当时所有银行都遭遇挤兑，诸如此类的“热闹”情况。到了 1930 年代，人们就“银行体系和金融体系的正确架构到底应该是什么”展开了一场非常重大的辩论；当时一群经济学家提出了一个方案，叫做 Chicago Plan。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And the kind of ringleader was a Chicago economist.
Actually, it might have been a Yale economist or Princeton at the time, but Irving Fisher, who wrote a book called 100% Money.
And that idea was that full reserve money was essentially, you know, government obligation money.
So, it's still the obligation of the government, like the US government in that instance.
But that essentially, you can have that, and you can hold that, but you can't take that and then fractionally lend against it.

paragraphs.zh: 这个方案的核心带头人是一位 Chicago 经济学家。实际上，他当时也可能是 Yale 或 Princeton 的经济学家；这个人是 Irving Fisher，他写过一本书，叫做 100% Money。这个思想的核心是：全额准备金货币本质上是一种政府负债货币（government obligation money）。所以，它仍然是政府的负债，比如在那个例子里就是 US government 的负债。但关键在于，你可以持有这种货币，却不能再拿它去进行部分准备金式的放贷。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So, you have kind of a full reserve, and you can only lend full reserve money.
And so, that was a big proposal for how to structure the way the financial system worked.
And it was actually lobbied very, very hard against it by the banks.
And the banks really liked fractional reserve.

They liked to be able to have the inherent kind of leverage and risk taking, and instead convinced the government to establish, or they collectively with the government sanctioned, established an insurance company called the Federal Depository Insurance Company Corporation.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，你拥有的是一种全额准备金，而你也只能贷出全额准备金货币。因此，这曾是一个关于如何构建金融体系运作方式的重大提案。实际上，银行业曾非常、非常强烈地游说反对它。银行很喜欢部分准备金制度；它们喜欢那种内生的杠杆能力和风险承担空间，随后又说服政府设立——或者说它们与政府共同批准设立——一家名为 Federal Depository Insurance Company Corporation 的保险机构。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so, that was a kind of corporate insurance model, but the risk taking still existed.
And so, we've continued to kind of face those issues.
The great financial crisis was an example of 30x leverage, 12x leverage, 14x leverage against these sort of base layers.
And so, my philosophy has been, well, right now, in terms of general utility, our existing economic system, it does depend on really major reserve currencies like the dollar.
And my view is like that's going to continue for a while.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，那是一种公司化的保险模型，但风险承担依然存在。因此，我们后来也一直在持续面对这些问题。The great financial crisis 就是一个例子：人们在这些基础层（base layers）之上做了 30 倍杠杆、12 倍杠杆、14 倍杠杆。所以，我的理念一直是：就当前的一般效用而言，我们现有的经济体系确实依赖像 dollar 这样的主要储备货币（reserve currencies），而我的看法是，这种情况还会持续一段时间。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Maybe thirty, forty, fifty years, it'll continue for a while.
But what we want to do is construct a system that is in fact safer.
So, full reserve form of money.
And that's what stablecoins are.
That's what dollar stablecoins are.

paragraphs.zh: 也许会持续三十年、四十年、五十年，总之还会持续一段时间。但我们想做的是构建一个事实上更安全的系统，也就是一种全额准备金形态的货币。而 stablecoin 就是这样，dollar stablecoins 也是这样。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And in fact, with the Genius Act that passed last year, it's sort of codified in law.
Like, you can't do anything with this.
It's like this very narrowly bound, narrow money kind of model.
And so, I think in some ways, like that original vision, we've now got established in laws around the world.
And now we have to do more with it.

paragraphs.zh: 事实上，随着去年通过的 Genius Act，这一点某种程度上已经被写入法律了。也就是说，你不能拿这种钱去做别的事情；它是一种边界非常严格、非常狭义的狭义货币（narrow money）模型。所以我认为，从某种意义上说，最初的那个愿景，如今已经通过世界各地的法律被确立下来了。现在我们要做的是，基于它继续推进更多事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: We have to make it extraordinarily useful.
Can lend that form of money as well, but it's just that you can't do fractional reserve.

paragraphs.zh: 我们必须让它变得极其有用。你当然也可以贷出这种形式的货币，但关键是你不能实行部分准备金。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So what are stablecoins currently backed by?
My understanding is, for example, the stablecoin companies are big buyers of treasuries, or US treasuries, and other sort of instruments like that.
Could you explain a bit more sort of what tends to back these things?

paragraphs.zh: 那么，stablecoins（稳定币）目前究竟是由什么来支撑的？我的理解是，例如，stablecoin 公司是 treasuries（国债），或者说 US treasuries（美国国债），以及其他类似工具的大买家。你能不能再多解释一点，这类东西通常是由什么来作为支撑的？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
So, up until really the last couple of years, you know, stablecoins like USDC had to be always one for one redeemable against, you know, very safe liquid assets.
And we couldn't take risk outside of what was permitted under the kind of payment system laws that regulated us.
And so that was Circle.
There are other people who didn't take that approach.

paragraphs.zh: 对。实际上，一直到最近这几年之前，像 USDC 这样的 stablecoins，都必须始终能够按照一比一赎回，对应的是非常安全、流动性很强的资产。而且我们不能承担超出监管我们的那类支付系统法律所允许范围之外的风险。Circle 一直是这么做的。也有其他一些人没有采取这种做法。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And, you know, but, you know, sort of fiat stablecoins in this way, we're back that way.

Now, laws have now come into play in major jurisdictions, whether it's in Europe, or Japan, or The US, etc. And we've been following whatever laws apply to us, you know, whenever they apply to us, obviously.

But what that's really led to is an architecture, which is basically what's now federal law, which is really holding only short duration US government treasuries, or treasury collateral that is overnight with like global banks.
So, that's, you know, very safe overnight treasury collateral for cash.

paragraphs.zh: 不过，像这样的 fiat stablecoins（法币稳定币），我们的支撑方式就是这样。现在，主要司法辖区都已经有相关法律开始生效了，不管是在 Europe、Japan，还是 The US 等等。显然，只要有适用于我们的法律，我们都会遵守。但这实际上形成了一种架构，而这种架构现在基本也已经体现在 federal law（联邦法律）里：本质上就是只持有短久期的 US government treasuries（美国政府国债），或者存放在全球性银行、以隔夜方式持有的 treasury collateral（国债抵押品）。所以，这基本上就是非常安全的、以现金对应的隔夜国债抵押品。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And then, you know, some amount in cash that is for kind of immediate liquidity.
But in that case, it's sort of holding it in these sort of big custodial institutions like Bank of New York that holds hundreds of trillions of dollars, etc, of assets.
And so, that is essentially the architecture of USDC.
And we're very transparent.
We have daily transparency onto most of it through a system we set up with BlackRock.

paragraphs.zh: 另外，还会持有一定数量的现金，用于即时流动性。但在这种情况下，这些资金通常是存放在像 Bank of New York 这样的大型 custodial institutions（托管机构）里，这类机构托管着数百万亿美元的资产，等等。所以，这本质上就是 USDC 的架构。我们也非常透明。通过我们和 BlackRock 搭建的一套系统，对于其中大部分资产，我们都能做到按日透明披露。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So USDC is

paragraphs.zh: 所以 USDC 就是——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: like a crypto token that anybody can effectively purchase, and in exchange for $1 you'd get one USDC.
And the USDC continues to be backed by a government treasury, like a short term T Bill or some cash or some exchange.

paragraphs.zh: ——一种 crypto token（加密代币），基本上任何人都可以购买；你支付 1 美元，就会得到 1 个 USDC。而这个 USDC 会继续由政府国债来支撑，比如短期 T Bill（短期国库券），或者一些现金，或者一些 exchange。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: That's right.
It's backed by treasuries, repos, and short duration T bills.
The average duration tends to be of the T bills, that portfolio tends to be around like thirteen days.
So, it's super, super liquid and kind of it sort of allows it to be treated as like a cash instrument.

paragraphs.zh: 没错。它由 treasuries（国债）、repos（回购协议）和短久期 T bills（短期国库券）支撑。T bills 那部分投资组合的平均久期通常大约在 13 天左右。所以，它的流动性非常、非常强，因此基本上可以被当作一种现金工具来对待。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: What do people do with it?
What are the

paragraphs.zh: 人们会拿它来做什么？有哪些——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: main use cases of USDC?
The conception of this obviously is like a general protocol for dollars on the internet.
And in fact, the whole design is this is like a general purpose, general architecture money.
And we actually see it used, you know, from at the very smallest end, like someone who's paying, you know, 25¢ for a digital object in a digital game that's built on a blockchain.
That would be like one end.

paragraphs.zh: USDC 的主要 use case（使用场景）是什么？它的基本构想显然是把美元做成互联网上的一种通用协议。实际上，它的整体设计就是一种通用用途、通用架构的 money（货币）。而且我们确实看到它被用在各种场景里：最小的一端，比如有人在一个基于 blockchain（区块链）构建的数字游戏里，为一个数字物品支付 25¢。这算是一端。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Or even now we're starting to see, and we'll come back to this topic I'm sure, you know, AI agents that are paying for the output of essentially the AI tokens of another AI agent.

And they're, you know, spending, again, just, you know, a dollar, 50¢, 20¢, etc. So, super tiny transactions at one end, all the way to the largest electronic trading firms in the world that do huge amounts of capital markets activity who are, you know, settling multi $100,000,000 transactions.
And the powerful thing is it's all the same.

Just like, you know, if I send you an email, you know, and my email's like, hey, this is what I had for breakfast, the payload of that is the same as if I sent you an email that had like a CAA dossier attached to it.

paragraphs.zh: 甚至现在我们开始看到——我相信我们等会儿还会回到这个话题——AI agent（智能体）在为另一个 AI agent 输出的、某种意义上的 AI token（代币）付费。它们花的钱同样很小，比如 1 美元、50¢、20¢ 等等。所以，一端是极其微小的交易，另一端则是全球最大的电子交易公司，它们进行大规模资本市场活动，并结算数亿美元的交易。而强大之处在于，这一切都是同一种东西。就像，如果我给你发一封 email（电子邮件），内容是“嘿，这是我早餐吃的东西”，它的 payload（载荷）和我给你发一封附带 CAA dossier 的 email，本质上是一样的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like, USDC doesn't care.
You know, so as general architecture, it can be used across a huge range of things.

And we have everything from merchants in Stripe and Shopify that are using it, to Visa actually using it themselves to actually move money on their own internal network instead of using the legacy banking system, to lots of kind of neobanks, remittance companies that are using it as a way to move value.
You know, a great kind of B2B fintech ramp just yesterday launched USDC as like core to their treasury system.
You can use to pay invoices, pay annual payroll.

paragraphs.zh: USDC 并不在乎这些。所以，作为一种通用架构，它可以被用于极其广泛的场景。我们看到从 Stripe 和 Shopify 上使用它的 merchant（商户），到 Visa 实际上亲自使用它，在自己的内部网络中转移资金，而不是使用 legacy banking system（传统银行体系）；再到很多 neobank（新型银行）、remittance company（汇款公司）把它当作转移价值的一种方式。就在昨天，一家很出色的 B2B fintech 公司还推出了以 USDC 为 treasury system（财资系统）核心的方案。你可以用它支付 invoice（发票），支付年度 payroll（工资）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So my sense is some of the reasons people do this is number one, you can do it at any time.

So for example, if I send a wire, I know a lot of crypto companies, for example, that when they raise money, they ask you to send USDC because instead of hitting a wiring deadline in the afternoon, can wire the money on the weekend.
You can send money anytime.

paragraphs.zh: 所以我的感觉是，人们这么做的一些原因，第一是你可以在任何时间进行操作。比如说，如果我发一笔 wire（电汇），我知道很多 crypto 公司在融资时会要求你发送 USDC，因为这样你就不用赶下午的电汇截止时间，周末也能把钱打过去。你可以随时汇款。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Which just works the way the internet works, right?
I mean, our expectation is like, I can pick up, you know, my WhatsApp, or I can pick up my WeChat, and I can just communicate in video with anyone anywhere, and it just works, Right?
And my expectation is like, hey, if I make a piece of software, and I put it on the Internet, billions of people can access it.
I don't need to do something special.
I think that's basically, this is just Internet native, and it runs on Internet protocols.

paragraphs.zh: 这就像互联网本来该有的工作方式，对吧？我的意思是，我们的预期是：我拿起 WhatsApp，或者拿起 WeChat，就可以和世界上任何地方的人进行视频沟通，而且它就是能用，对吧？而我的预期也是：如果我做出一段软件，并把它放到 Internet 上，数十亿人都可以访问它，我不需要做什么特别的事。我觉得本质上就是这样：这是 Internet native（原生于互联网）的，它运行在 Internet protocol（互联网协议）之上。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so it behaves the way that any piece of data or content behaves on the Internet, which is what our expectations are, people's expectations are.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 所以它的行为方式，就和互联网上任何数据或内容的行为方式一样，而这正是我们的预期，也是人们的预期。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I was just trying to enumerate a little bit of what makes it a superior instrument for all sorts of purposes.
And one is 20 fourseven accessibility, Two, maybe some form of transaction fees relative to the volume.
And then three is, my sense is it's also a way for people to participate in US dollars who often would not have access directly.
And so they use crypto as almost a proxy too.

paragraphs.zh: 我刚才只是想稍微列举一下，是什么让它在各种用途上都成为一种更优的 instrument（工具）。第一，是 24/7 的可访问性；第二，可能是相对于交易规模而言的某种 transaction fee（交易费用）优势；第三，我的感觉是，它也让那些通常无法直接接触美元的人，能够参与到 US dollars（美元）之中。所以他们几乎是把 crypto（加密资产）当作一种 proxy（替代入口）来使用。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah, for sure.
I mean, I think store value is a really big thing, and we see that.

And in fact, like the law that was passed last year, the Genius Act, like a big motivation for the administration, and this is something that we've been proposing and kind of pushing for a long time is that this is a way to continue to export the dollar.
And so, we're now exporting digital dollars, and we're doing that all around the world.
And that's like strategically important to The United States from a geopolitical, geoeconomic perspective.

paragraphs.zh: 对，当然。我觉得 store of value（价值储存）是一个非常大的点，我们也确实看到了这一点。事实上，去年通过的那部法律，Genius Act，政府推动它的一个重要动机——而这也是我们长期以来一直在提出并推动的事情——就是把这看作一种持续输出美元的方式。所以现在，我们输出的是 digital dollars（数字美元），而且是在全球范围内这么做。从 geopolitical（地缘政治）和 geoeconomic（地缘经济）角度来看，这对 The United States 具有战略重要性。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: But you know, there's other things too, which is, this comes from my own background as well, which is, this is programmable money.
There's never been programmable money.
Like, you actually have essentially, like our stablecoin network is just a public API on the public internet that anyone can plug into and use.
And so, if I'm a developer, and I want like global dollar settlement, and I want to provide that as a capability to my users, I don't have to ask permission.
I can just go connect to that smart contract, connect to that public API, and boom, I have now an application with Global Digital Dollar Utility.

paragraphs.zh: 不过你知道，还有别的东西，这也来自我自己的背景，那就是：这是 programmable money（可编程货币）。以前从来没有过 programmable money。实际上，本质上，我们的 stablecoin（稳定币）网络就像公共互联网上的一个公共 API，任何人都可以接入并使用。所以，如果我是一个开发者，我想要全球范围的 dollar 结算，并且我想把这种能力提供给我的用户，我不需要请求许可。我只需要去连接那个 smart contract（智能合约），连接那个公共 API，砰一下，我现在就有了一个具备 Global Digital Dollar Utility 的应用。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so that's really different.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 所以这真的非常不一样。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And smart contracts is basically a way to write code that's wrapped around this money that allows you to effectively have a virtual contract online.
So you can say under XYZ conditions, pay this out.
I'm going to generate a financial instrument off of this.
And it's just kind of based on this other layer that you can plug into effectively.

paragraphs.zh: 而 smart contracts 基本上是一种把代码写在这种货币外层的方式，它能让你实际上在线上拥有一种虚拟合约。于是你可以说，在 XYZ 条件下，支付这笔钱。我会基于此生成一种金融工具。它基本上就是建立在另一个你实际上可以接入的底层之上。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
That is definitely the case.
And I think the idea of programmable money was like, again, this early idea that we had.
And smart contracts was sort of the original expression.
But when I looked at that thirteen years ago, my view was that blockchain networks are operating systems.

paragraphs.zh: 对，情况绝对是这样。我觉得 programmable money 这个想法，还是我们当年最早的想法之一。而 smart contracts 算是它最初的表达形式。但在十三年前我看这个问题时，我的观点是：blockchain networks（区块链网络）是 operating systems（操作系统）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: They're going to be operating systems.
And so, when we think about operating systems, we have lots of paradigms for that.
We have mobile operating systems.
The web was itself kind of an operating system with a runtime and a language model and an object model.
And, you know, clouds became kind of like these big virtual operating system environments.

paragraphs.zh: 它们将会成为 operating systems。所以，当我们思考 operating systems 时，我们已经有很多现成的范式。我们有 mobile operating systems（移动操作系统）。Web 本身在某种程度上也是一种 operating system，它有 runtime（运行时）、language model（语言模型）和 object model（对象模型）。而且，你知道，clouds（云）后来也有点像这种大型的虚拟 operating system 环境。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: AI foundation models are now essentially operating systems that execute tasks and other things.
Blockchains are operating systems, and they have compute engines.
They have virtual machines, and you can write Turing complete code.
You can write software that runs on these.
But there's some really key attributes that make them different.

paragraphs.zh: AI foundation models（AI 基础模型）现在本质上也成了 operating systems，用来执行任务和其他事情。Blockchains（区块链）是 operating systems，它们有 compute engines（计算引擎）。它们有 virtual machines（虚拟机），而且你可以编写 Turing complete（图灵完备）的代码。你可以写在这些系统上运行的软件。但有一些非常关键的属性让它们与众不同。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So, the first is that the code is sort of tamper resistant.
Once it's published, it's sort of like out as like a machine that's tamper resistant.
The second is it's perfectly auditable.
You can audit every single input and output of that machine, of that code in real time.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，第一点是，这些代码某种意义上是 tamper resistant（防篡改）的。一旦发布，它就像一台具备防篡改能力的机器，被放到了外面。第二点是，它是完全可审计的。你可以实时审计那台机器、那段代码的每一个输入和输出。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Because it's all on a public blockchain, so anybody in the world can That's right.

paragraphs.zh: 因为这一切都在 public blockchain（公共区块链）上，所以世界上任何人都可以——没错。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So, it's like all the compute is public, accessible, it's open source by nature, and that's really powerful as well.
And, you know, it also has these sort of essentially kind of transaction and compute integrity assurances.

And this is really key, and it ties back to AI as well, which is like you want assurances that the machine is doing what it said it's going to do, and you want kind of the inputs and outputs to be provable, and the state of the machine to be provable.
And these are things that it was not easy to do in the past.
And so, network computers, these operating systems now provide for that.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，这就像是所有 compute（计算）都是公开的、可访问的；它天然就是 open source（开源）的，这一点本身也非常强大。而且，你知道，它还提供了某种本质上的 transaction（交易）与 compute（计算）完整性保障。这一点非常关键，也和 AI 有关：你希望能保证机器确实在做它声称要做的事；你也希望输入和输出是可证明的，机器的状态也是可证明的。而这些事情在过去并不容易做到。所以现在，network computers（网络计算机）以及这些 operating systems（操作系统）已经能够提供这些能力。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And as we're moving into the AI driven economic system, right, having those mechanisms becomes even more important.
It happened to be important for financial transactions where integrity proof, auditability, verifiability are like intrinsic in a fiduciary apparatus.
That was like really key.
But now, when we're dealing with, you know, kind of autonomous actions in the economy, that also becomes extremely It'd

paragraphs.zh: 而随着我们进入 AI 驱动的经济系统，对吧，拥有这些机制就变得更加重要了。它们原本在金融交易中就很重要，因为 integrity proof（完整性证明）、auditability（可审计性）、verifiability（可验证性）本来就是 fiduciary apparatus（受托金融体系）中的内在要求。这一点一直都非常关键。但现在，当我们处理经济中的 autonomous actions（自主行为）时，这也会变得极其——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: be great to talk about that because, you know, jeez, probably seven, eight years ago, me and my friends used to speculate that the most likely place maybe that AGI would emerge, which again, I don't think is going be the case in the future, would be off of the blockchain because you had these effectively agents.
They're very simple agents you've been running back then in some sense in terms of doing transactions on the blockchain.
And you had these economic games that were multi turn economic games to some extent that these actors could play.
And so we said, isn't that a great place to basically evolve intelligence?
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 很想聊聊这个，因为，天哪，大概七八年前，我和朋友们曾经推测，AGI 最有可能出现的地方，也许会是在 blockchain（区块链）上——当然，我现在并不认为未来会是这样——因为那上面实际上有这些 agents（智能体）。它们在当时某种意义上是非常简单的 agents，只是在 blockchain 上执行交易。与此同时，还存在一些某种程度上的 multi-turn economic games（多轮经济博弈），这些参与者可以去玩这些博弈。所以我们当时会说，这难道不是一个非常适合让 intelligence（智能）进化出来的地方吗？对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Because you have these multi turn games, you have economic incentives, you have game theory, you learn all sorts of lessons off of that.
Obviously, there's a very different world now with sort of generative AI and foundation models.
But I'd love to hear your view of where are Agenic payments going?
And is it going to be crypto?
Is it going to be more just traditional banking systems?

paragraphs.zh: 因为你有这些多轮博弈，你有经济激励，你有 game theory（博弈论），你能从中学到各种各样的经验。当然，现在的世界已经因为 generative AI（生成式 AI）和 foundation models（基础模型）而非常不同了。不过我很想听听你的看法：Agenic payments（智能体支付）会走向哪里？会是 crypto（加密货币）吗？还是会更多是传统银行系统？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Is it a hybrid?
Like, what do you think are the drivers of that?
I mean, there's a lot

paragraphs.zh: 会是 hybrid（混合模式）吗？比如，你觉得驱动这一切的因素是什么？我的意思是，这里面有很多——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: in there.
There's lot we could talk about.
So, maybe first, like, I think, you know, my own view is that, you know, we're going through a pretty steep kind of curve right now.

We're like in the, you know, about three months into a pretty dramatic shift in kind of the fundamental capabilities of technology, probably the most dramatic that I've ever seen in my own time in technology.
And I think, you know, that shift is effectively going to mean that a couple things in my view.

paragraphs.zh: 这里面内容很多。我们可以聊的也很多。所以，也许先说第一点：我自己的看法是，我们现在正处在一条相当陡峭的曲线上。我们大概才刚进入一种相当剧烈的转变三个月左右，这种转变发生在技术的基础能力层面，可能是我自己从事科技以来见过最剧烈的一次。我认为，这种转变实际上会意味着几件事。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So, the first is that more and more of the actual work that is done in the real economy, especially in, you know, kind of the, what we call the white collar economy.
But in many, many areas of service and delivery and so on, like so much of that is going to be conducted by AI agents.

And so, AI agents conducting the work, AI agents collaborating with each other, AI agents, you know, consuming services from each other and kind of, you know, purchasing effectively specialized intelligence or output, etc. Like, we're on a really interesting curve there.
And so, the kind of agentic economy is being born as we speak.

paragraphs.zh: 第一，是现实经济中越来越多的实际工作，尤其是在我们所说的 white collar economy（白领经济）里，但也包括大量服务、交付等领域，其中非常多的工作都会由 AI agents 来完成。也就是说，AI agents 在执行工作，AI agents 彼此协作，AI agents 从彼此那里消费服务，并且实际上在“购买”专业化的智能或输出，等等。我们正处在一条非常有意思的曲线上。所以，agentic economy（智能体经济）此刻正在诞生。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And in that world, we need a different infrastructure for the financial intermediation layer.
Why?
Well, we don't have an infrastructure that can support that.
We don't have an infrastructure that can work globally, interoperably, instantly, that can be programmed through software layers by arbitrary pieces of software.
That doesn't exist.

paragraphs.zh: 而在那个世界里，我们需要一套不同的基础设施，来承载 financial intermediation layer（金融中介层）。为什么？因为我们现在并没有能够支持它的基础设施。我们没有一种基础设施，能够在全球范围内运作、实现 interoperably（互操作）、即时完成，并且能够被任意软件通过 software layers（软件层）进行编程。这种东西目前并不存在。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: We need an infrastructure where the agents themselves can dynamically create and spin up different kind of financial endpoints themselves.
We need transactions that can scale potentially into the billions or trillions of transactions.
We don't have that.
We also the ability to kind of handle transactions at micro scale as well.
So, you know, for example, consuming a certain amount of intelligence might be $05 or $00 as it is with ease.

paragraphs.zh: 我们需要一种基础设施，让 agent 自身能够动态地创建并启动各种不同类型的金融端点。我们需要能够扩展、潜在支持数十亿甚至数万亿笔交易的交易系统。现在我们还没有这样的东西。我们还需要具备在微观尺度上处理交易的能力。比如说，消费一定量的 intelligence（智能）可能只要 $05 或 $00，而且这一切都能轻松完成。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so we need that to work.
We need that to work in real time, again, between any piece of hardware, software, anywhere

paragraphs.zh: 所以我们需要它真正运作起来。我们需要它能够实时运作，而且同样要能在世界上任何地方的任何硬件、软件之间运行。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: in the world.
Isn't it all about those stuff that people have been talking about for a long time in terms of just crypto?
Like the benefits of crypto?

paragraphs.zh: 这不就是人们长期以来谈论的那些东西吗，也就是 crypto（加密货币）所谓的那些好处？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: It hasn't really become possible until really just the last couple of years.
So, it really took kind of third generation blockchains to actually deliver on this.
So, now today, like, you know, you actually can look at like transaction volumes of USDC, which is by far the most transacted digital currency in the world, way more than anything else.
And transaction volumes have grown incredibly.
And off of like a monetary base, it's also growing, but the transaction volumes are growing way faster.

paragraphs.zh: 但实际上，直到最近这几年，这件事才真正变得可行。所以，确实需要到第三代 blockchain（区块链）才能真正兑现这一点。比如说今天，你可以去看 USDC 的交易量，它是迄今为止全球交易最活跃的 digital currency（数字货币），远远超过其他任何东西。而且它的交易量增长得极其惊人。它所依托的 monetary base（货币基础）也在增长，但交易量的增速要快得多。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And that's because money velocity has picked up.
The cost to transact is now subsent reliably.
And so, when you take out the cost, you can do more transactions.
And so with Arc, which we can come back to, we now have an infrastructure where we can conduct transactions for a millionth of a penny, which just was never feasible

paragraphs.zh: 这是因为货币流通速度上来了。现在交易成本已经能够稳定地降到低于 1 美分。于是，当你把成本拿掉之后，你就能进行更多交易。再加上 Arc——这个我们之后可以再展开——我们现在已经有了一种基础设施，能够以一美分的百万分之一来完成交易，这在以前根本不可行。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: before.
Yeah, tell us more about Arc, because I know you folks are rolling this out as your blockchain, etcetera.
Would just love to learn more about what it is, what are the use cases, what differentiates I

paragraphs.zh: 以前根本做不到。对，跟我们多讲讲 Arc 吧，因为我知道你们正在把它作为自己的 blockchain 推出来，等等。我很想更了解它到底是什么、有哪些 use cases（使用场景）、以及它的差异化在哪里。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: would love to talk about that.

I want to finish one other thought on this sort of authentic piece, which is, I think in addition to this sort of like financial infrastructure that's needed in this world, and the role that will play, and it ties back to your actual kind of question and stuff that you were thinking about before, my own view is that, you know, agents and seeing what happened with OpenClaw, and MoltBook, and all this stuff is all really interesting, because it showed that you could actually see emergent forms of cooperation, of interaction, of engagement amongst AIs.
And that's pretty powerful.
And clearly, like we're at the front edge of that.
Like, there's going be a lot more of that.

paragraphs.zh: 我很乐意谈这个。不过我想先把关于“真实性”这部分的另一个想法讲完。我认为，除了这个世界所需要的那类金融基础设施，以及它将发挥的作用之外——这也回到了你刚才真正提出的问题，以及你之前在思考的那些事——我自己的看法是，agent 以及像 OpenClaw、MoltBook，还有这些东西所展示出的现象，都非常有意思，因为它们表明，我们实际上已经能够看到 AI 之间涌现出的合作形式、互动形式和参与形式。这一点非常有力量。很显然，我们现在还只是站在这个趋势的最前沿。接下来还会出现更多这样的情况。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so, if you have AI agents that are from around the world, they could be generated from lots of different models, and LLMs, and the like, and they need to kind of coordinate, they need a medium, a trustworthy medium where they can do that, where they can instantiate an entity, where they can store value in that entity.
They can execute and arrange contracts that intermediate the work and the tasks, and that where all of it is real time mathematically and computationally provable.
And so blockchain infrastructure now actually gives us the building blocks for, when I say agentic economic activity, most people think, oh, that's e commerce or payment is not.

Agentic economic activity is actually how does the organization of what we used to think of as labor and capital, but essentially like kind of how does this organization of kind of compute work happen, and what kinds of corporate forms might emerge in that world to do that.
And so, I'm actually quite interested in that.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，如果你有来自世界各地的 AI agents，它们可能由许多不同的模型、LLM（大语言模型）之类生成，并且它们需要进行协调，那么它们就需要一种媒介，一种值得信赖的媒介，让它们能够做到这一点；在这个媒介中，它们可以实例化一个实体，可以把价值存储在这个实体里；它们可以执行并安排合约，让这些合约在工作和任务之间起到中介作用；而且这一切都必须是实时的，并且在数学和计算上可证明。于是，今天的 blockchain 基础设施实际上已经为我们提供了这样的 building blocks（构件）。当我说 agentic economic activity（agent 驱动的经济活动）时，大多数人会想到“哦，那就是 e commerce（电子商务）或支付”，但其实不是。所谓 agentic economic activity，真正指的是：我们过去称为 labor（劳动）和 capital（资本）的组织方式，将如何被重新组织；更准确地说，这种 compute（算力/计算）工作将如何被组织起来；以及在那样的世界里，可能会涌现出什么样的 corporate forms（公司形态）来完成这件事。所以，这其实是我相当感兴趣的问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: That does tie to ARC, because that's a design surface that we care about, which is basically, we describe ARC as an economic operating system.
And this goes back to a comment I made earlier, which is these networks are operating systems.
And we're moving now from the kind of like early adopter era, which you're very familiar with, which was mostly around like, you know, speculation on different things.
There were some interesting things like NFTs or whatever.
But like, we're now moving very squarely because of stablecoins into like the real economic activity side of this.

paragraphs.zh: 这确实和 ARC 有关，因为那是我们关心的一个设计层面。基本上，我们把 ARC 描述为一个 economic operating system（经济操作系统）。这也回到我前面提到的一点：这些网络本身就是操作系统。现在我们正从你非常熟悉的那种 early adopter（早期采用者）时代走出来；那个阶段大多围绕着对各种东西的投机，当然也有一些有意思的东西，比如 NFTs，诸如此类。但现在，由于 stablecoins（稳定币）的推动，我们正在非常明确地转向它的真实经济活动这一侧。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And I think my view is that as we go forward, the substance of what we think of as contracts, the substance of what we think of as corporations, are going to be software machines themselves.
And so, we're going to see this progression.

And so, ARC as an economic operating system is conceived of as a compute environment for laying down all of the building blocks of economic activity, whether that's storing value moving money, or instantiating a corporate form, or manifesting and intermediating complex contracts.
Like a lot of this stuff, which was conceptual a long time ago, is now like real.
And we have a legal basis for it.

paragraphs.zh: 我认为，展望未来，我们今天所理解的 contracts（合同）的实质，以及我们所理解的 corporations（公司）的实质，都会变成软件机器本身。所以，我们会看到这样一种演进。正因如此，ARC 作为一个 economic operating system（经济操作系统），被构想为一个 compute environment（计算环境），用来铺设经济活动的所有基础构件——无论是存储价值、转移资金，还是实例化一种公司形态，或者呈现并中介复杂合同。很多这类很久以前还只是概念性的东西，现在都已经变成现实了，而且我们也有了相应的法律基础。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: We have regulatory clarity for it increasingly.
And what's interesting is that the drivers of this machine economy are actually machines.
And so, you know, our view is, you know, Arc is designed for this moment, which is a moment when machines are going to play a larger and larger role in all of the output of the economic system.

paragraphs.zh: 我们也越来越多地获得了监管上的明确性。更有意思的是，这种 machine economy（机器经济）的驱动力实际上就是机器本身。所以，我们的看法是，ARC 正是为这个时刻而设计的——这是一个机器将在整个经济系统产出中扮演越来越大角色的时刻。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So if I look at a lot of the blockchains that people have found exciting over the last few years, obviously there's Bitcoin, which, you know, was almost purposefully designed in a certain way to make it a little bit less adaptable to all these new things that are happening now.
Yeah.
Know, Solana, Ethereum, etc. Have been in the past the traditional places that people thought about ways to build smart contracts, to build a lot of the types of things that you just described.
What do you think is the difference between some of these more traditional L1s or blockchains and sort of what you're doing at ARC?

paragraphs.zh: 如果我看过去几年人们觉得很令人兴奋的许多 blockchain（区块链），显然有 Bitcoin；它当初几乎是有意被设计成某种样子，以至于面对现在正在发生的这些新事物时，它的适应性会稍弱一些。然后像 Solana、Ethereum 等等，过去一直是人们传统上会想到的地方，用来构建 smart contracts（智能合约），以及你刚才描述的很多那类东西。你认为，这些更传统的 L1 或 blockchain，和你们在 ARC 所做的事情之间，区别是什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
So, a few big things.

I think the first is that, you know, as I think you were sharing, or we were talking about before we started recording, but like, you know, I think a lot of the designs on blockchains from, let's call it, the early adopter phase, a lot of it was sort of like, hey, we're going to build something that is completely censorship resistant, or outside of the reach of governments.
It's sort of like we're building an alternative universe.
And that's like the goal.

paragraphs.zh: 对，有几个大的不同。我觉得第一点是，正如我想你刚才提到的，或者说我们在开始录音前聊到的，blockchain 在所谓 early adopter（早期采用）阶段的很多设计，某种程度上都像是在说：我们要构建一个完全 censorship resistant（抗审查）的东西，或者一个处于政府触及范围之外的东西。有点像是在构建一个 alternative universe（平行宇宙），而那就是目标。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And I think, you know, decentralization is itself a good goal.

But I think as we move from early adopter to sort of mainstream scaling, where, you know, whether it's, you know, a major company like Walmart, or it's, you know, a household that's thinking about like how they store their wealth, the intermediaries, and there will continue to be intermediaries, we're not all going to be your own bank.
The intermediaries have obligations in terms of the kind of like robustness of the infrastructure that they have to run.
And so ARC is actually set up with a number of features.
One is that it's actually a known validator set.

paragraphs.zh: 我认为，decentralization（去中心化）本身当然是一个好的目标。但我觉得，当我们从 early adopter 走向某种 mainstream scaling（主流规模化）阶段时——无论是像 Walmart 这样的大公司，还是一个在考虑如何存储自身财富的普通家庭——中介机构依然会存在，而且会继续存在；我们不可能人人都成为自己的银行。这些中介机构在其必须运行的基础设施稳健性方面是有义务的。所以 ARC 实际上为此设置了若干特性，其中之一就是，它采用的是一个已知的 validator set（验证者集合）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so the infrastructure operators of ARC are major financial infrastructure companies.
And those are companies that are held to these very high standards for InfoSec compliance, reliability, availability.
What are some examples

paragraphs.zh: 因此，ARC 的基础设施运营方是一些大型金融基础设施公司。这些公司都必须满足非常高的标准，包括 InfoSec（信息安全）合规性、可靠性、可用性。能举几个例子吗

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: of some of the validators on your network?

paragraphs.zh: 比如说，你们网络上的一些 validators（验证者）有哪些？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So, we haven't announced the validators yet, but that will come in due course.

But, you know, the model at a high level is that you have financial companies, financial infrastructure companies, including possibly like large technology companies that are responsible for running the infrastructure.
So, it's a distributed infrastructure.
But because of that, we're able to provide assurances.
We're able to provide assurances that like the bad guys aren't running your transactions.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，我们目前还没有公布 validators，但会在适当时候公布。不过你知道，从高层来看，这个模型是由金融公司、金融基础设施公司，甚至可能还包括大型科技公司来负责运行基础设施。因此，它是一个分布式基础设施。但正因为如此，我们才能提供保障。我们能够提供这样的保障：运行你交易的不是坏人。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And we can also run assurances that transactions actually have settlement finality.

paragraphs.zh: 我们还能够保证交易实际上具有 settlement finality（结算终局性）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Like, they

paragraphs.zh: 比如说，它们

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: can't be hard forked.
They can't be reorged.
And so, you can get what's called deterministic settlement finality.
And that's really important, whether it's a security, or a piece of cash, or whatever it is, in essentially hundreds of milliseconds.
And that's really important.

paragraphs.zh: 不能被 hard fork（硬分叉），不能被 reorg（链重组）。因此，你可以获得所谓 deterministic settlement finality（确定性结算终局性）。而这一点非常重要——无论对应的是证券、现金，还是其他任何东西——基本上都能在数百毫秒内完成。这非常重要。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: The other piece is that it's sort of designed with real money as the foundation.
So, there's not like a volatile gas token.
USDC is actually the default native token, which is now, under the law, essentially like a legal form of electronic money.
So, you have real dollars as the way that people understand.
And so, to a company that's like doing this, it's like, I pay AWS credits.

paragraphs.zh: 另一部分在于，它的设计是以 real money（真实货币）作为基础的。所以这里不存在那种价格波动的 gas token。USDC 实际上是默认的原生 token，而且按照现行法律，它本质上已经是一种合法的电子货币形式。因此，你面对的是人们所理解的真实美元。对一家正在做这件事的公司来说，就像是：我在支付 AWS credits。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I understand how to budget for that, my treasury, my operations, my compliance, etc. So, this allows basically for like actual usage to make sense both to the user, to the developer, to the corporations, the FIs that deal with this.
So, that's really important.

And then I think the other is like, we've been building in a lot of primitives that are important to like the way that payment systems work, the way that capital markets work, the way that, you know, the privacy requirements that are needed in some of these cases, but still allowing for like compliance to happen.
So, we've kind of purpose built this for a different kind of set of participants who need to run on it.

paragraphs.zh: 我知道该如何为此做预算，我的 treasury（资金管理）、operations（运营）、compliance（合规）等等都知道怎么处理。所以，这基本上让实际使用在用户、开发者、企业，以及与之打交道的 FIs（金融机构）层面都变得合理可行。这一点非常重要。然后我认为另一点是，我们已经内建了很多重要的 primitives（基础组件），它们对于支付系统的运作方式、资本市场的运作方式，以及某些场景中所需要的 privacy（隐私）要求都很关键，同时仍然允许 compliance（合规）得以实现。所以，我们某种程度上是为一类不同的参与者专门打造了这个系统，他们需要在上面运行。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And we've had the advantage as Circle of working with many of the leading financial institutions that are getting into this space over the last couple of years, whether it's the Visas, or the BlackRocks, or the Bank of New York Mellons, or all these types of companies.

So, we've been able to work with them, and we've been able to work with governments around the world to hear like, hey, like central bankers all over the world, like what's important as you think about like allowing this internet infrastructure to run the financial system?
And we're kind of trying to incorporate in a lot of the kind of requirements in the sense that they have.
And so that's very different.
I think it's a different design space.

paragraphs.zh: 而作为 Circle，我们的优势在于，过去几年里我们一直在与许多进入这一领域的领先金融机构合作，不管是 Visa、BlackRock，还是 Bank of New York Mellon，或是其他这类公司。所以，我们能够与他们合作，也能够与世界各国政府合作，去听取他们的看法——比如全球各地的 central bankers（央行官员）会认为：如果要让这种互联网基础设施运行金融系统，什么才是重要的？我们正在尝试把他们所理解的很多这类要求纳入进来。所以这非常不同。我认为这是一个不同的设计空间。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And I think, you know, these new distributed network operating systems will need to support like the real economy's activity, not a kind of shadow economy.
And so, that's just substantively different.
There are a lot of other technical things I could talk about that are part of it.
But those are, I think, helpful just in terms of framing this.

paragraphs.zh: 而且我认为，这些新的分布式网络操作系统需要支持真实经济活动，而不是某种影子经济。因此，这在本质上就是不同的。关于其中涉及的其他很多技术点，我也还可以讲很多。但我觉得，至少从理解这个问题的框架来说，这些点已经是有帮助的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: What else do you think is interesting that's happening in the crypto world today outside of stablecoins and sort of related infrastructure?
Because I know that there was a whole wave of things that people were doing.
On the infrastructure side, was ZK rollups, there's a variety of approaches over the last few years.
Besides stablecoins, is there anything that you think was especially interesting, or that will be impactful, or a lot of these things kind of infrastructure looking for solutions?
I'm sort of curious how you think about crypto writ large right now.

paragraphs.zh: 除了 stablecoins 以及某种相关基础设施之外，你觉得当今 crypto 世界里还有什么正在发生、并且很有意思？因为我知道，之前有一整波人在做各种事情。在基础设施这一侧，比如有 ZK rollups，过去几年里也出现了各种不同的方法。除了 stablecoins 之外，有没有什么是你觉得特别有趣、或者会产生重要影响的？还是说，这里面很多东西某种程度上都只是看起来像基础设施、但还在寻找应用场景？我有点好奇，你现在是怎么从整体上看待 crypto 的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
I mean, look, I think there's a lot of attention that has gone into kind of what I'll broadly call kind of scaling models.

And so, if you take as your kind of design center that these are network computers, and these network computers are really good at establishing record keeping that is kind of public and available to all, and to perform computing on those records that's public and available to all.
As general utility space, that's like super, super interesting.
And so, a lot of this has been like, okay, well, how do we make sure that that can scale?

paragraphs.zh: 是的。我的意思是，你看，我觉得现在有很多注意力都投向了我会宽泛地称为 scaling models（扩展模型）的方向。所以，如果你把它们的设计中心理解为 network computers（网络计算机），而这些网络计算机真正擅长的是建立一种公开、所有人都可访问的记录保存机制，以及对这些公开、所有人都可访问的记录执行计算。作为一种通用基础设施空间，这件事就非常、非常有意思。所以，这里面很多工作其实都在围绕一个问题展开：好，那我们怎么确保它能够扩展？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so, as an example, right, in a world of like billions of AI agents that are swarming and doing other things, like scaling this is actually extremely important.

So, ZK, you know, roll ups as an example, or zero knowledge proofs more broadly as a way for proving compute, which allows you to do compute off chain and then prove it to the on chain, that's actually really important.
So, these sort of off chain, or trusted execution environments, and other things that provide cryptographic proofs of compute, or other kind of assertions becomes really key.
So, actually, a lot of that research is now becoming extremely valuable.
The same thing goes for a lot of that research and development is critical to enabling privacy.

paragraphs.zh: 举个例子，在一个有数十亿个 AI agents（AI 代理）四处协作、执行各种任务的世界里，这种扩展能力实际上极其重要。所以，以 ZK rollups 为例，或者更广义地说，zero knowledge proofs（零知识证明）作为一种证明计算的方法，它让你可以在 off chain（链下）完成计算，然后再把证明提交到 on chain（链上），这实际上非常重要。因此，这类 off chain 方案、trusted execution environments（可信执行环境），以及其他能够提供计算的 cryptographic proofs（密码学证明）或其他断言的技术，就变得非常关键。所以，很多这方面的研究现在实际上正变得极其有价值。同样，这其中的大量 research and development（研发）工作，对于实现 privacy（隐私）也至关重要。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so, want all the benefits of kind of open, interoperable, kind of permissionless infrastructure, but we also want to be able to have privacy.
Like corporations don't want everyone to see what they do, or we don't want to be doxxed, and like all this kind of stuff.
And so, that's now coming into real production.
That was like research y for a long time.
Like, ARC day one is shipping with like built in privacy primitives, which is again the result of a lot of work for a long time.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，我们既想要 open（开放）、interoperable（可互操作）、permissionless（无需许可）的基础设施所带来的各种好处，但同时我们也希望能够拥有 privacy。比如公司并不想让所有人都看到它们在做什么，我们自己也不想被 doxxed（身份信息被曝光），诸如此类。所以，这些东西现在正真正进入生产环境。很长一段时间里，那都更像是研究课题。比如，ARC 从第一天开始交付时就内置了 privacy primitives（隐私原语），而这再次是长期大量工作的结果。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so those are important pieces.

And then, you know, I think as kind of more large scale financial infrastructure comes over to this, as we move, you know, where the New York Stock Exchange or the biggest derivatives clearinghouses are like, yeah, we're going to move to an on chain world, like the scaling stuff becomes really, really critical.
And so, I actually feel like now more than ever, like those big work streams coming online.

And, you know, if I use as a reference point, like, you know, I spent a long time building on the early internet, the early 90s, and the early web, and like all this stuff all the way up until like 2001 for like, you know, for me, it was like ten years.
And it was still, it was like awful still.

paragraphs.zh: 所以这些都是重要的组成部分。然后，你知道，我觉得随着更大规模的金融基础设施开始迁移到这上面，随着我们走向这样一个世界——比如 New York Stock Exchange，或者最大的衍生品清算机构，会说“是的，我们要迁移到 on chain 世界”——那么这些扩展能力相关的东西就会变得极其、极其关键。所以，我实际上感觉现在比以往任何时候都更像是：那些大型工作流正在真正上线。而且，如果拿我自己的经历做个参照，我在早期互联网、90 年代早期以及早期 web 上做了很长时间，一路做到了 2001 年左右——对我来说，差不多有十年——可当时它依然还是很糟糕。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like, it just like kept grinding, and it was like, how do we make this useful?
How do we make this useful?
How do we make this useful?

And then you had, you know, a whole bunch of things happen that were in the background, like, you know, Wi Fi, broadband, you know, you finally got like usable other internet connected devices, and you could actually really start to do stuff.
So, you could actually deliver software over the internet.

paragraphs.zh: 就像，它一直在艰难推进，大家反复在问：我们怎么让这东西变得有用？我们怎么让这东西变得有用？我们怎么让这东西变得有用？然后，后台陆续发生了一整批事情，比如 Wi Fi、broadband（宽带），你终于有了真正可用的其他联网设备，然后你才真的开始能够做事情。这样一来，你才真正可以通过互联网交付软件。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: You could actually deliver media over the internet.
You could actually do communications, like real time communications over the internet.
But it was like ten years in the desert or longer before you could even get there.
And I kind of feel that way about the blockchain space.

Like, it's been a dozen years or so, and now we're kind of having like the broadband moment, and the demands of society and the financial the agentic economy, all of that is sort of coming together at a really interesting time.

paragraphs.zh: 你才真正可以通过互联网交付媒体。你才真正可以通过互联网进行通信，比如实时通信。但在到达那一步之前，你经历了像是在沙漠里走了十年，甚至更久。我对 blockchain 领域也有类似的感觉。它已经发展了大概十二年左右，而现在我们某种程度上正迎来属于自己的 broadband 时刻；与此同时，社会需求、金融体系以及 agentic economy（代理型经济）的需求，都在一个非常有意思的时间点上开始汇合。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I guess one thing that

paragraphs.zh: 我想有一件事就是

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: people have been talking about for a long time in the crypto world is securitization of other assets under the blockchain.
Yeah.
And so that would be stock.
So should you be able to buy fractions of Berkshire Hathaway using crypto?
And should that be globally available?

paragraphs.zh: 在 crypto 领域，人们长期以来一直在讨论的一件事，就是把其他资产在 blockchain 上做 securitization（证券化）。对。比如 stock（股票）。所以，用户是否应该能够用 crypto 购买 Berkshire Hathaway 的零碎份额？而且这是否应该面向全球开放？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Given the success of USDC and other stablecoins, that's made it even more interesting in terms of a provable model.
When do you think that stuff will happen, and what approach do you think will be taken, and how It's does that tie into the Ozempic

paragraphs.zh: 鉴于 USDC 和其他 stablecoin（稳定币）的成功，这件事从一个可被验证的 model（模型）角度来看就变得更有吸引力了。你觉得这类事情会在什么时候发生？你认为会采取什么样的 approach（路径）？以及它和 Ozempic 有什么联系？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: totally happening.
There's a great site, if people are interested, called rwa.xyz.
Real world assets is sort of what that refers to, RWA, xyz.
I'm very proud because there are tokenized stocks that are out there, and the most active tokenized stock today is not Tesla.
It's not the S and P index.

paragraphs.zh: 这完全正在发生。如果大家感兴趣，有一个很好的网站叫 rwa.xyz。RWA 指的是 real world assets（现实世界资产），xyz 就是那个域名。我很自豪地说，现在市场上已经有 tokenized stocks（代币化股票）了，而当下交易最活跃的 tokenized stock 不是 Tesla，也不是 S and P index。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: It's actually Circle.
So, that was cool to see.

paragraphs.zh: 实际上是 Circle。所以，看到这一点很酷。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's cool.

paragraphs.zh: 这很酷。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: We also, you know, have seen this growth in like tokenized money markets.
So, basically like on chain treasury bills.
We actually operate the largest tokenized treasury product called USYC, like US yield coin.
That's grown quite fast as well.
And we run the largest tokenized euro as well, EURC.

paragraphs.zh: 我们也看到，像 tokenized money markets（代币化货币市场）这样的东西在增长。所以，基本上就是 on-chain（链上）的 treasury bills（国库券）。我们实际上运营着规模最大的 tokenized treasury product（代币化国债产品）之一，叫 USYC，也就是 US yield coin。它增长得也相当快。此外，我们还运营着规模最大的 tokenized euro（代币化欧元），也就是 EURC。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so, we're definitely like looking at this broadening out.
I think there's a huge effort right now at every layer of the whole financial system stack to go into tokenization.

So, all the way down at the layer of like the people that keep the records of the stock, which is like, you know, if you're familiar, like the computer shares of the world, or companies of the world, up to like the layers that like are the depository clearing systems, like the DTCC, which, you know, most people don't know, but it's actually like the backplane of how all securities work.
Like they're moving to tokenize.
And then the actual like brokers and exchanges want to take those and support those tokens, and trading on those tokens, and distribution of those tokens.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，我们肯定也在关注这件事的进一步扩展。我认为，现在整个金融系统 stack（技术栈）的每一层都在大力推动 tokenization（代币化）。从最底层那些负责保存股票记录的机构开始——如果你熟悉的话，就像全球那些 computer shares 这一类公司——再到像 depository clearing systems（存托清算系统）这样的层级，比如 DTCC。大多数人并不知道，但它实际上是整个 securities（证券）运作方式的 backplane（底层主干）。这些机构都在转向 tokenization。然后，实际的 brokers（券商）和 exchanges（交易所）也希望接入这些 token（代币），支持这些 token 的 trading（交易）和 distribution（分发）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So, NASDAQ, New York Stock Exchange, all of them, they're all doing this as we speak.
And as we speak, the SEC has been providing clear guidelines on how to do that.
And so, they actually issued guidance just about a month or so ago that basically said, here's what you do in all these layers.
Here's your obligations.
And so, we're at a point where like technology and then the market's desire is creating that.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，NASDAQ、New York Stock Exchange，它们所有机构此时此刻都在做这件事。而且就在现在，SEC 也一直在就如何开展这件事提供明确的 guidelines（指引）。事实上，大约就在一个月前，他们刚发布了相关 guidance（指导意见），基本上就是说：在所有这些层级里，你们应该怎么做；你们各自的 obligations（义务）是什么。所以，我们现在正处在这样一个节点：technology（技术）以及 market（市场）的需求，正在共同推动这件事发生。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And right now, the interesting thing about things like tokenized stocks is mostly it's interesting to enable people not in The US to access these.
That's where a lot of the growth has happened, because not everyone has access.
Used go

paragraphs.zh: 而且现在，像 tokenized stocks 这样的东西，真正有意思的地方主要在于，它能让不在 The US 的人也获得这些资产的访问渠道。很多增长都发生在这里，因为并不是每个人都有 access。过去也有过

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: the other way, right?
There used to be Chinese stocks that were basically held in third party instruments that you could purchase on stock exchanges so you could participate in some of the Chinese listed Right.

paragraphs.zh: 反过来的情况，对吧？以前有一些 Chinese stocks，基本上是通过第三方工具持有的，而你可以在 stock exchanges 上购买这些工具，这样你就能参与到部分在 Chinese 上市的——对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
Know.
I mean, that's definitely some of the packagers of like ETFs and funds and stuff have kind of mirroring for sure.
But, you know, I think like this is similar to like, you know, when we went through like the web becoming available or broadband really hitting the scale.
A lot of times people just think, oh, I have this existing product.

paragraphs.zh: 对。我明白。我的意思是，这当然有点像某些 ETF、funds 之类产品的打包方，确实会做某种镜像映射。但你知道，我觉得这有点类似于当年 web 开始普及，或者 broadband 真正达到大规模覆盖的时候。很多时候，人们只是会想，哦，我已经有这个现成的产品了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I can now put it over here.
Like, here's the TV show.
I'm going to put the TV show over here.
I think what becomes a lot more interesting, or here's the game.
It's this game that used to be on a CD, and now you can download it or whatever.

paragraphs.zh: 我现在可以把它搬到这边来。比如，这里有个 TV show，我要把这个 TV show 放到这边。我觉得真正变得更有意思的是——或者比如这里有个 game，这个 game 以前是在 CD 上的，现在你可以下载它，诸如此类。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I think the really interesting thing is like, what can you do that you couldn't do before?

What kind of utility gets unlocked, whether it's fractionalization, or how you can borrow and lend on these things, or how you could package them together in different ways and enable And AI could play a pretty significant role in that

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得真正有意思的是：你现在能做哪些以前做不到的事？到底解锁了什么样的 utility（效用）？无论是 fractionalization（碎片化持有），还是你如何围绕这些资产进行 borrow 和 lend，或者你如何用不同方式把它们打包组合起来并加以实现——而 AI 在这里也可能扮演相当重要的角色。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: as Sounds like you could really tie into some of the prediction market stuff as well that's been happening.
Because to some extent, the world's biggest prediction markets are actually stock markets, or financial markets, I should say, more broadly.

paragraphs.zh: 听起来，这似乎也可以和最近一直在发展的 prediction market 相关的东西真正结合起来。因为从某种程度上说，世界上最大的 prediction markets，其实就是 stock markets，或者更广义地说，是 financial markets。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
Prediction markets themselves are becoming kind of parallel infrastructure for people who participate in stock markets, right?

In fact, the biggest adopters, it seems, of the market makers of prediction markets are actually the people who are trying to figure out what is reality, and what does that mean for companies and equities and stuff and the interplay or whatnot.
And yeah, I mean, we're seeing that.
I mean, USDC powers Polymarket, for example.

paragraphs.zh: 对。prediction markets 本身，正在变成参与 stock markets 的人所使用的一种平行基础设施，对吧？事实上，看起来 prediction markets 里最大的 adopters，或者说 market makers，实际上正是那些试图弄清“现实到底是什么”、以及这对 companies、equities 之类东西意味着什么，并研究其中相互作用的人。对，我们确实看到了这一点。比如说，USDC 就为 Polymarket 提供支持。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so, the same guys that are, you know, trading derivatives over here on, you know, oil or Bitcoin over here are also like, I'm moving my money quickly using USDC over here to like figure out what's going to happen in some event.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，同一批人一边在这边交易 oil 或 Bitcoin 的 derivatives，一边也会在另一边用 USDC 快速转移他们的资金，去判断某个事件接下来会发生什么。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's cool.
The one other thing that I think has been happening a little bit recently is there's been a couple papers that have been focused on basically tying proof of work into just generic inference work.

paragraphs.zh: 挺酷的。我觉得最近还稍微发生了一件事，就是已经有几篇论文在关注，基本上是把 proof of work 和通用的 inference（推理）工作绑定起来。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yes.
In other words, can

paragraphs.zh: 对。换句话说，能不能

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: you tie those two things so you're being very GPU efficient in terms of what you're doing, but also you can effectively generate incremental revenue through GPU usage while you're using it for inference for other

paragraphs.zh: 把这两件事结合起来，让你在所做的事情上对 GPU 的使用非常高效，同时在把 GPU 用于其他 inference 工作时，也能够通过 GPU 的使用有效地产生增量收入。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah, I'm really interested in And I think, again, the little conversation we were having before we recorded, like, you know, proof of work obviously was itself an innovation, and sort of essentially like the exhaust of the proof of work of Bitcoin is just like the exhaust of energy consumption.
And so, doesn't actually In some ways, it's waste, in a sense.
The energy is waste.
So, I think the idea of essentially like inference compute as GPU, inference compute as proof of work.

And so the work itself is the inference, and that as the underlying basis for proof of work cryptocurrency is pretty interesting, and would be, you know, potentially something that could align with the kind of monetary principles of something like Bitcoin, but actually be productive proof of work.

paragraphs.zh: 对，我对此真的很感兴趣。而且我觉得，还是回到我们开始录制前那段简短对话，比如说，proof of work 显然本身就是一种创新，而 Bitcoin 的 proof of work 某种本质上的“尾气”，其实就只是能源消耗的“尾气”。所以从某种意义上说，它实际上是一种浪费。被浪费的是能源。所以，我认为，把 inference compute 作为 GPU 的工作负载、把 inference compute 视为 proof of work 的想法很有意思。这样一来，工作本身就是 inference，而这又成为 proof of work cryptocurrency 的底层基础，这相当有趣。它可能会与 Bitcoin 这类事物背后的货币原则相契合，但同时又是一种真正具有生产性的 proof of work。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: That's really interesting.
And so, you know, my own view, and this is like, you know, I think goes back a long time, is like the, you know, people have kind of axiomatically sort of assumed like, well, Bitcoin is the thing.
It got the network effects.
It has all of this.
And I've always said like, I don't know what we're going be using in ten years.

paragraphs.zh: 这真的很有意思。所以，你知道，我自己的看法是，而且这也是一个由来已久的看法：人们某种程度上一直把“Bitcoin 就是那个答案”当成公理化前提来接受——它已经获得了 network effects（网络效应），它拥有这一切。而我一直都在说，我不知道十年后我们会用什么。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like, we don't know.
Now, Bitcoin has lasted a really long time.

But I think like the paradigm shift that we're seeing in energy infrastructure, in the performance of, you know, the conversion of energy into intelligence, and the compute layers, that, it certainly opens up a new avenue to think about this that wasn't readily available fifteen years ago.
So, if you

paragraphs.zh: 比如，我们并不知道。现在，Bitcoin 的确已经持续了很长时间。但我认为，我们正在看到的 energy infrastructure（能源基础设施）、把能源转化为 intelligence（智能）的性能，以及 compute layers（计算层）方面的范式转移，确实打开了一条新的思考路径，而这是十五年前并不现成存在的。所以，如果你

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: were to give one piece of advice to agents, it would not be buy Bitcoin.

paragraphs.zh: 要给 agents（智能体）一条建议，那不会是去买 Bitcoin。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Just joking.
I have a

paragraphs.zh: 开个玩笑。我有一个

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: better question.
Terms of, say we were to think out ten years, what does that world look like in your mind?
And obviously, we're going through a period of intense change.
I'm finding it incredibly hard to predict the future right now in terms of just what's going happen in AI, much less AI plus crypto plus the global economic system plus everything else.
So, given all that and putting that aside, yeah, exactly, what is your vision of the future?

paragraphs.zh: 这是个更好的问题。比如说，如果我们把时间拉到十年后，在你脑海里那个世界会是什么样子？显然，我们正处在一个剧烈变化的时期。就现在而言，光是 AI 会发生什么，我都觉得未来已经极其难以预测了，更不用说 AI 加上 crypto，再加上全球经济体系以及其他一切因素。所以，考虑到这些、也暂且把这些放在一边的话，对，没错，你对未来的愿景究竟是什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
Well, I mean a couple things I would say.
The first is sort of the thing that everyone is debating right now is the pace of AI diffusion, right?
So, what is the pace of AI diffusion?
And what does that then imply in terms of the kind of amount of change that we're going to have to deal with?

paragraphs.zh: 对。嗯，我想我会说几件事。第一点，其实就是现在所有人都在争论的那个问题：AI diffusion（AI 扩散）的速度，对吧？所以，AI diffusion 的速度到底有多快？而这又意味着，我们将不得不应对多大规模的变化？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so, that's all debatable, right?
You hear Dario debating that versus others and so on.
But like, it definitely feels like the diffusion limiters are, in some cases, bureaucratic, in some cases legal, in some cases human risk, or other things, right?
But we these limiters that are there.
But it does seem like the pace of diffusion is accelerating and will continue to accelerate.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，这些都是可以争论的，对吧？你会听到 Dario 和其他人在争论这个问题，等等。但总的来说，确实感觉 diffusion（扩散）的限制因素在某些情况下是 bureaucratic（官僚层面的），某些情况下是 legal（法律层面的），某些情况下是 human risk（人类风险），或者其他因素，对吧？也就是说，这些限制因素确实存在。但看起来，diffusion 的速度正在加快，而且还会继续加快。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: That's pretty dramatic.

So, I guess like my own view, it's very rooted in my own like political and economic philosophy, is that we have a real opportunity to create essentially new social, political, and economic organizational structures.
And in many ways, like we have to.

There's a kind of, you know, in these periods, whether it's the Enlightenment and the Industrial Revolution and other things, like there's these periods where there's like a new definition of the social contract.
And that new definition of the social contract is then in turn reflected in social, political, and economic ordering, and the mechanisms that we use for those things.

paragraphs.zh: 这相当具有冲击性。所以，我想，我自己的看法其实非常植根于我个人的政治与经济哲学：我们确实有一个真正的机会，去创造本质上全新的社会、政治和经济组织结构。而且在很多方面，我们也必须这么做。在这种时期里——无论是启蒙运动、工业革命，还是其他类似阶段——都会出现一种对 social contract（社会契约）的重新定义。而这种对社会契约的新定义，随后又会体现在社会、政治和经济秩序之中，以及我们为这些事务所使用的机制之中。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And it feels like to me like we're going to forced through that.

And I think, you know, that's simultaneously like terrifying and exciting, etc. And I am of the view that we are going to have a kind of lag effect between the disruption and the establishment of those new institutional forms.
But at the same time, I actually believe like new institutional forms are going to be emergent out of this.

And so, as I talked about earlier, like the formation of these kind of on chain organizations that have different forms of governance and contracting a mixture of human and agentic actors, like that seems like we're going have a lot more of that.

paragraphs.zh: 而在我看来，我们将会被迫经历这一过程。我觉得，这同时又是可怕的、令人兴奋的，等等。我的判断是，在 disruption（颠覆、扰动）发生之后，到这些新 institutional forms（制度形态）真正建立起来之间，会存在一种滞后效应。但与此同时，我实际上相信，新的 institutional forms 会从这一过程中涌现出来。正如我之前提到的，这类 on-chain（链上）组织的形成，拥有不同形式的治理与契约机制，并由 human（人类）和 agentic actors（agent 式行动者）混合构成——看起来这种形态将会大量增加。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: We're going to probably have huge proliferation of that.
And it may be that those corporate forms are like the most productive corporate forms that we've ever seen in economic history.
And then, you know, they'll need to be kind of like an overlay into the governance systems of, you know, political organizations and systems as well.
And it, yeah.
So, you know, my view is we're going to have simultaneously all around the world a renegotiation of the social contract.

paragraphs.zh: 我们很可能会看到这种形态的大规模 proliferiation（激增、扩散）。而且也许这些 corporate forms（公司形态）会成为经济史上我们见过的生产力最高的公司形态。然后，你知道，它们也需要以某种叠加层的方式进入 political organizations and systems（政治组织和系统）的治理体系之中。对，所以，我的看法是，接下来全世界会同时展开一场对 social contract（社会契约）的重新谈判。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And it's going require new systems of participation in economics and governance that we haven't had.
That's like very high level mumbo jumbo y, but it's also, you know, sort of how I think about it at a high level.

paragraphs.zh: 而这将要求我们建立全新的经济参与和治理参与系统，是我们过去从未拥有过的。这个说法听起来有点非常高层次、甚至有点 mumbo jumbo（玄而又玄），但这也确实是我在高层面上思考这个问题的方式。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's super interesting.
Have you ever read a book called Lady of Mazes?
No. It's like a sci fi book from, I don't know, fifteen years ago about the post ATI world.

And part of it is as a big enough block or demographic emerges in human society, an overlay AI agent that's observing everything spawns a specific agent that represents that viewpoint that then is part of this sort of virtual senate of agents negotiating policies.

paragraphs.zh: 这特别有意思。你读过一本叫 Lady of Mazes 的书吗？没有。那大概是一部十五年前的 sci-fi（科幻）小说，讲的是 post ATI world（ATI 之后的世界）。其中有一部分设定是：当人类社会中出现了一个足够大的群体或人口板块时，一个观察一切的覆盖式 AI agent（AI agent，智能体）就会生成出一个专门代表该观点的 agent，而这个 agent 随后会成为某种由 agents 组成的虚拟 senate（元老院/议会）的一部分，一起协商政策。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So it's kind of this interesting view of how can you spontaneously spawn these sorts of systems from a governance perspective, which is kind cool.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，从治理（governance）的视角看，这提供了一种很有意思的观察：这些类型的系统如何能够自发地涌现出来，这还挺酷的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I would love to read that.

paragraphs.zh: 我很想读一读那个。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: What is your prediction?
And so if you look at a lot of prior ways of technology, their actual impact to GDP has been difficult to tease out.
Right?
So the productivity gains of the internet versus actual GDP growth and things like that have been notorious.
And there's all sorts of reasons for that.

paragraphs.zh: 你的预测是什么？如果你回头看很多以往的技术，它们对 GDP 的实际影响一直都很难剥离出来，对吧？比如 internet 带来的生产率提升，与实际 GDP 增长之间的关系，以及类似这些问题，一直都是出了名地难以厘清。而这背后有各种各样的原因。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It could be measurement, could be deflationary aspects of some of these things, it could be a variety of things.
How do you think about the GDP impact of AI?
So if you think ahead five years, what do you think the global economy is?
10 bigger, 50% bigger, three times the size?
Does that even matter as a metric anymore?

paragraphs.zh: 可能是测量问题，也可能是这些东西某些方面带有通缩效应（deflationary aspects），也可能是很多别的因素。你怎么看 AI 对 GDP 的影响？如果把时间往后看五年，你觉得全球经济会是什么样？大 10 倍、大 50%，还是变成现在的三倍？或者说，GDP 作为一个指标，到那时还重要吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I mean, like I see this debate all the time, and Kathy Woods talking about, you know, we're going to have 10% GDP growth for the 2030s.

paragraphs.zh: 我的意思是，这种争论我一直都能看到，比如 Kathy Woods 在说，到 2030 年代我们会有 10% 的 GDP 增长。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah, sure.

paragraphs.zh: 对，当然。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: You know, etcetera.
I don't quite know what to think.

I mean, I think, you know, it's quite plausible that the kind of giant leaps that we see in kind of productive output in a huge range of industrial to other commercial services, etc, like really drives a very significant discontinuous jump in GDP at an absolute level.
It'll have, in many ways, probably less meaning than we've historically had with GDP.

And, you know, the kind of economic well-being indexes that we think about, like GDP will be, you know, the risk here is that GDP effectively, the GDP growth is a sort of capital capturing more capital at the expense of humans.

paragraphs.zh: 诸如此类吧。我其实不太确定该怎么想。我的意思是，我觉得相当有可能的是：我们在大范围的工业生产到其他商业服务等领域看到的那种生产性产出的大幅跃升，确实会在 GDP 的绝对水平上推动一次非常显著、非连续式的跳升。不过，在很多方面，它的意义可能会比历史上 GDP 所代表的意义更小。还有，我们所考虑的那类经济福祉指标，比如 GDP，这里的风险在于：GDP 增长实际上可能只是资本以牺牲人类利益为代价攫取更多资本的一种表现。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like, that's the real risk.
And so, like GDP growth generally has been like a really great thing.
And so the question is, is like, will it remain a great thing?
Do we have the new social contract to deal with that yet?

But I guess my general view, just sort of as a technologist talking about, you know, seeing what we see with diffusion and kind of other changes, like it does feel like we have the potential for double digit GDP numbers in the 2030s.

paragraphs.zh: 这才是真正的风险。所以，GDP 增长通常一直都是一件非常好的事。问题在于，它还会继续是一件好事吗？我们现在有与之相适应的新社会契约（social contract）了吗？不过，如果只是作为一个 technologist 来谈，基于我们在 diffusion 以及其他一些变化中所看到的情况，我的总体看法是：到 2030 年代，我们确实有可能看到两位数的 GDP 增长数字。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like that seems not unrealistic to me.
Not that that's gonna be uniform all around the world, but certainly in large large parts of the world, that seems very achievable based on what I see.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得这听起来并不不现实。当然，这不可能在全球各地都完全一致，但至少在世界上非常非常多的地区，按照我所看到的情况，这似乎是完全可以实现的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Amazing.
Thank so you much for joining us today at No Priors.
Super interesting conversation.
Thank you.

paragraphs.zh: 太精彩了。非常感谢你今天来到 No Priors。这个对话特别有意思。谢谢你。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3
